caption.text: In the 1960s, the city of Boston razed and redeveloped the blocks between Castle, Dover,  Tremont Streets, and Shawmut Avenue into a public housing project called Castle Square as part of a series of “slum clearing” endeavors. This dramatically oversimplifies the story of this area. In the early twentieth century, this part of Boston’s South End was densely populated and very mixed-use. It included multiple schools, both religious and secular, as well as two theaters and many places of worship. Let’s take a tour of the area through the decades before redevelopment.

We’ll start with this building in 1902. Boston Latin School was founded in the 1600s as the first public school in what would become the United States. The English High School was founded in 1821, an early public high school in the country. Both schools only admitted boys until 1972, and both have moved around the city over time. For the years between 1866 and 1944, the schools shared a building, moving to this location together in 1881, and were generally seen as counterparts. Boston Latin was a college preparatory school while The English High School specialized in educating working-class boys for business, mechanics, and engineering trades.

This area had long been targeted by reformers, even before the slum clearing efforts of the 1960s that lead to the Castle Square public housing project. These efforts included the first public school lunch program which was introduced by the New England Kitchen, an 1890s reform effort that originally centered around selling cheap but healthy food that was made based on newly discovered concepts of nutrition to working class families for them to eat at home. This original concept was based elsewhere in Boston, but when they shifted their focus to industrial-scale cooking, like for the schools, they consolidated operations closer to some of their main clientele. Where was this new base of operations?

In 1902, this unassuming building at 485 Tremont Street was the operational center for the newest iteration of the New England Kitchen. Alongside the school lunches, they also provided huge amounts of beef broth to hospitals in Boston, as well as some other large-scale applications of their methods. Just looking at this map, it is hard to understand the impact this building had on the local community.
This is what the interior of the building looked like during its time as the base of operations for the New England Kitchen's school lunch distribution.
Moving on from the historic schools, this building near the Latin and English High Schools has an oddly circular footprint within it because two decades earlier…
…it was originally built as the Battle of Gettysburg Cyclorama.

The building housed a massive circular painting depicting the Battle of Gettysburg in hyper-realistic detail in order to fully immerse the viewer in the painting, as if they were at the battle personally. As this photograph shows, it was a very popular form of entertainment at the time…
…So popular in fact that a second one, depicting the Battle of Bunker Hill, was constructed just blocks away.
Unlike the first cyclorama, this building would remain an entertainment site for the next several decades.

The Castle Square Theater, named after the square on which it was located, was built in 1894 with an adjoining hotel. The hotel, also named after the square, would have helped attract theatergoers traveling from farther away. It also likely served as a place to temporarily house the performers who were part of the traveling opera company based out of this theater

The Castle Square Theater and Hotel had a striking façade, made to mimic a triumphal arch. At six stories high, the building also stood higher than most of the surrounding 4-5 story buildings, and it had the sign on the roof that reached even higher.

When the square was renamed after Arlington Street around 1920, the theater and hotel were renamed with it. In this 1922 map, they appear as the Arlington Theater and Hotel Arlington respectively. [The theater briefly switched to showing motion pictures in the late 1910s](https://cinematreasures.org/theaters/15773), but by the time of this map, they had returned to live theater.
In this 1931 photo taken from a distance, the tall profile and façade of the hotel and theater are recognizable, dominating the square they sat upon.
The theater was ornate, able to hold about 1000 audience members, and it retained the circular wall and roof of the cyclorama it was built around.

The theater and hotel could not survive the Great Depression, so over the winter of 1932-33, the building was demolished. Many photographs exist of the demolition, but in this shot, the structure of the domed roof going all the way back to the cyclorama is visible.
By 1938, the lot the theater and hotel sat on was vacant. In the atlases these maps come from, vacant spaces are left white while buildings are generally pink or yellow.
This area had been the home of railroads into and out of Boston dating back quite early in the history of railroads in the United States.
In 1861, there was just one railroad line going through this part of the South End…

…By 1938, there were many. Two major stations were built in the decades between these two maps, Back Bay Station to the west and South Station to the northeast. This would have contributed to a noisy and grimy environment along the railways, as trains during this time period were all coal-fired.
So who lived in this area that would be demolished and redeveloped? The places of worship and names of building owners can give us a clue.
On Emerald Street, we see an Albanian Orthodox church across the street from a Jewish synagogue.

This church, while unassuming on the page, has a significant place in the history of Orthodoxy in the United States. It was the first Albanian church established in the United States, and it was established by Theofan Stilian Noli, who was an Albanian scholar, clergyman, and political activist educated in Boston. He briefly served as Prime Minister of Albania in 1924 before returning to the United States.

Over the course of the Great Depression and World War II, more Albanians moved to the United States, and they began to form an Albanian Diocese. In 1949, the congregation of the church on Emerald Street moved to this cathedral in South Boston, becoming St. George Albanian Orthodox Cathedral.

The Cathedral is a striking red brick building on East Broadway. It is now the headquarters of the Albanian Orthodox Archdiocese in America. Image: Jameslwoodward, [CC BY-SA 3.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0), via Wikimedia Commons

According to [the history told by St. George Cathedral](https://www.saintgeorgecathedral.com/history1), during the Great Depression, fruit peddlers would park their carts on the street between these two places of worship, one Albanian Orthodox Christian and the other Orthodox Jewish.
On the next street over, we see two schools. The Wait School was a public elementary school, part of the South End's Franklin District, serving local families.

Information about the Wait School is hard to come by, but a [*Manual of the Public Schools of the City of Boston*](https://archive.org/details/manualofpublicsc1941bost/page/104/mode/2up) from 1941 lists the twelve people employed at the school. All of the teachers were women, and the only men employed were the custodian and the school physician. Physicians for the smaller elementary schools usually served the whole district, while the larger high schools sometimes had physicians who only worked in the one location.
Next to the Wait School was the Holy Trinity parochial German Catholic school, which was managed by the Archdiocese of Boston and was connected to the German Catholic cathedral across the street.

This area was heavily occupied by the Catholic Church. On one side of Shawmut Avenue is the German Catholic School, south of that was a convent, and across the street was, of course, Holy Trinity German Catholic Church.

Alongside the numerous schools in the area, the presence of a playground suggests many families with young children lived in this neighborhood. It is likely that this playground was used by children from both Wait and Holy Trinity schools, along with other children and families who lived in the area.

Now let's look at who lived and worked in this neighborhood. Close to redevelopment, the names on the buildings in this area suggest a very mixed-use and mixed population neighborhood. In these two blocks, we can see buildings owned by Good Samaritan Missions, the City of Boston, Brigham Hospital, and Fitchburg Art Asso. Inc. For individual owners, names from many different ethnic backgrounds appear: Joseph Batal, Hilda Alexander, Armand Poirer, and Esther Cohen, for example.
Just north of those two blocks is a veterinary hospital and a large garage owned by Railway Express Agency, Inc.

What justification did the city use to justify demolishing this neighborhood? According to a survey taken of the families displaced by the Castle Square housing project, the area was considered to be extremely rundown by the early 1960s, to the extent that [“Dover Street [was] the traditional main street of Boston’s Skid Row.”](https://archive.org/details/familyrelocation00much/page/n23/mode/2up)

This is a 1960 photograph of Dover Street, looking west from Washington Street, the same view from what we just saw on the map. The photo seems to tell a different story than “the main street of Skid Row” narrative, with its cars, cabs, delivery trucks, and abundant businesses. It was a working-class neighborhood that was cared for by those who lived and worked there. It was in the midst of Boston, likely noisy and grimy from several different factors, but it was nonetheless home to many people.

Let’s take one last look at that 1938 map. Here, several more buildings and lots are owned by the City of Boston, perhaps foreshadowing what was to come in this area. In all, the Castle Square housing project would displace nearly 2000 people in 782 household units, almost half of which were immigrant families, along with numerous businesses and religious communities.
